---
title: "PAGe: Phase-Aligned Gated Epidemic Forecasting"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    page-layout: full
    grid:
      body-width: 1300px
---

```{=html}
<style>

h2 {
  margin-top: 1.8em;
  padding: 0.4em 0.9em;
  border-left: 5px solid #1e5f8e;
  background: #e8f1f8;
  color: #1a3a5c;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
h3 {
  margin-top: 2em;
  padding: 0.4em 0.8em;
  border-left: 4px solid #4a90d9;
  background: #f4f8fd;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
h4 {
  margin-top: 1.5em;
  padding: 0.3em 0.7em;
  border-left: 3px solid #a0bfe0;
  background: #f9fbfe;
  border-radius: 0 3px 3px 0;
}
</style>
```

## Notation and Objectives

Let $s = 1,\dots,S$ index influenza seasons and let $w$ denote the within-season flu-week number. For each season–week $(s,w)$, define:

- $x_{s,w}$: number of influenza-positive tests (code column `y`)
- $n_{s,w}$: number of tests performed (code column `N`)
- $p_{s,w} = x_{s,w} / n_{s,w}$: observed weekly positivity (code column `p`)
- $\pi_{s,w}$: underlying probability of positivity (the quantity modelled/forecast)

Several week indices are kept in the data:

- `week`: MMWR **epidemiologic week-of-year** from surveillance data.
- `weekS`: **surveillance-week index** (season starts at MMWR week 35), for interpretation only.
- `weekF`: $w_s^f$, **flu-week index** (flu season starts at MMWR week `startWeek`; here 27).
- `iWeek`: $w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})}$, ignition week for season $s$ (manually labelled or estimated).
- `anchorWeek`: $w^{an}$, anchor week for alignment (median ignition week across seasons).
- `newWeek`: $w^a_{s,w}$, **aligned week index** after shifting $w^f_{s,w}$ so that $w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})}$ lines up at `anchorWeek` across seasons
- `peak`: $w_{s}^{p}$, week of the season's epidemic peak (estimated by alignment).

Let $P(s,w)\in\{0,1\}$ be a phase indicator:
$$
P(s,w)=
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{pre-ignition (pre-season)}\\
1, & \text{post-ignition (in-season)}
\end{cases}
$$

The framework has 3 main components:

- **$M_0$ (Ignition detector):** using only information available up to week $w$, estimate whether the season has ignited ($P(s,w)=1$), estimate reference curve.
- **$M_1$ (Alignment):** shifts and stretches the estimated reference curve to fit the observed data, producing an estimate of the current epidemic phase and a rough forecast trajectory.
- **$M_2$ (Forecast model):** once ignition is detected and aligned, build forecast model and forecast $h$ weeks ahead:
$$
\pi_{s,w+h} \;=\; \Pr\!\big(\text{positive at } w+h \mid P(s,w)=1,\, M_1\big).
$$

Primary forecasting targets: one- and two-week-ahead positivity $\pi_{s,w+1}$ and $\pi_{s,w+2}$. Raw surveillance data is loaded and converted to a within-season week index
(`weekF`) started at MMWR week 27. Each row is one season-week with columns `y` (positive tests), `N` (total tests), `p = y/N`. Seasons with known data quality issues (`2020-21`, `2021-22`) are excluded.


---

## $M_0$ — Ignition Detection {#sec-m0}

**Goal:** detect the start of the epidemic season ("ignition") in real time, so that alignment and forecasting can begin.

Ignition marks the transition from background noise to sustained epidemic activity. Detecting it accurately — and promptly — is critical: if ignition fires too late, M1 and M2 lose early-season data that anchors the alignment; if it fires too early, alignment parameters are estimated from noise. M0 uses a consensus-vote rule across four real-time gates, each capturing a different aspect of the signal (level, trend, cumulative burden, sustained elevation). All gates must agree simultaneously before ignition is declared.

### Training: Gate threshold tuning

The detector votes each week using four threshold-based gates built from data available only up to the current week:

| Gate | Signal | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| `cond_sum` | rolling sum of $p$ over $K_{\mathrm{sum}}$ weeks | sustained elevation, not a spike |
| `cond_p` | smoothed $\tilde p$ (window $L$) | current level above threshold $\tau_p$ |
| `cond_prev` | cumulative positivity $\mathrm{Prv}_{s,w}$ | season-wide burden exceeds $\tau_{\mathrm{prev}}$ |
| `cond_inc` | week-to-week direction of $\tilde p$ | signal not falling for $n_{\mathrm{consec}}-1$ steps |

Ignition fires when all four gates agree simultaneously for the first time within an eligible window $[w_{\min}, w_{\max}]$.

Gate thresholds $\theta$ are tuned via grid search with LOSO evaluation (using `alignedD` and known ignition weeks from historical data), minimising detection timing error while penalising late or missed detections. The tuned $\hat\theta$ is frozen for deployment.

**Key functions:** `tuneIgnitionGrid_M0v2()`, `detectIgnitionBySeason_M0v2()`

**Full analysis:** [`ignition_training.html`](ignition_training.html)

### Deployment: Weekly ignition checking

Once ignition locks at $\hat w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})}$, the alignment coordinate system activates:

$$
w^a_{s,w} = w^f_{s,w} - \hat{w}^{(\mathrm{ign})}_s + w^{an}.
$$

**Key function:** `run_ignition_weekly(currentSeason, params)`

---

## $M_1$ — Curve Alignment {#sec-m1}

**Goal:** fit observed epidemic curves to learned reference templates to estimate peak timing and provide short-term forecasts.

Once ignition is detected by $M_0$, historical seasons are aligned by their ignition weeks to estimate per-season and population **reference curves**. Each week during the live season, the current partial curve is aligned to multiple reference templates in parallel, and forecasts are **ensemble-weighted by alignment quality (NLL)**, yielding a continuously updated peak estimate.

**Peak tracking and alignment freeze:** Once the epidemic peak is detected (flagged as `peak_passed == TRUE`), alignment is **frozen** — no further parameter estimation occurs. This prevents over-fitting to the declining phase where the model has poor information, and provides a stable peak estimate for $M_2$ to anchor forecasts. The peak flag is tuned offline to minimize detection delay with zero false positives.

This whole process — reference curve estimation, prospective multi-template alignment, and peak detection — is validated and tuned via LOSO cross-validation.

### Reference curve estimation {#sec-refcurve}

A smooth seasonal template is built from historical data using **known (manually labelled) ignition weeks**. This becomes the foundation for all alignment and peak estimation.

Each season's ignition week $w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})}$ is assigned by expert review. Using these known labels, the data are aligned so all seasons are centred on their median ignition week $w^{an}$:

$$
w^a_{s,w} = w^f_{s,w} - w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})} + w^{an}.
$$

The reference curve is estimated using the **factor-smooth (fs) method**, which operates on aligned `newWeek` space:

**Method: `"fs"` (factor-smooth)**
- Uses a GAM term `s(newWeek, season, bs="fs")` with factor-smooth basis
- Returns per-season curves `eta_mat` (52 weeks × n_seasons matrix) for multi-template alignment
- Computes population reference as pointwise median across seasons
- Provides per-season flexibility while maintaining robustness to outlier seasons

`learn_alignment_hyperparams()` derives bounds and penalties (`TAU_BOUNDS`, `DELTA_BOUNDS`, `LAMBDA_DELTA`, `WEEK_THRESHOLD_DELTA`) stored as `hyper`.

**Key functions:** `flagIgnition(manual_labels=...)`, `alignIgnition()`, `estimateRef()`, `learn_alignment_hyperparams()`

**Full analysis:** [`estimateRef.html`](estimateRef.html)

### Alignment methods {#sec-m1-align}

The alignment uses a **4-parameter dilation model** on the logit scale. Two approaches are supported:

**Single-template baseline:** Uses a single population reference $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$:
$$
u_{s,w} = \frac{w^a_{s,w} - \tau_w}{1 + \delta_w}, \qquad \mathrm{logit}(\hat\pi_{s,w}) = a_w + b_w\cdot g_{\mathrm{ref}}(u_{s,w})
$$

**Multi-template ensemble (**selected**):** The FS reference method returns per-season curves $\eta_s(u)$ (columns of `eta_mat`). Multi-template alignment fits to each training season's curve independently:

1. For each training season $s'$, build a spline template from $\eta_{s'}$ and call `align_forecast_pipeline_dilate()` to estimate ($\tau_{s'}, \delta_{s'}, a_{s'}, b_{s'}$) and obtain NLL$_{s'}$
2. Compute softmax weights: $$w_{s'} = \frac{\exp(-\text{NLL}_{s'} / T)}{\sum_s \exp(-\text{NLL}_s / T)}$$ where $T$ is temperature (lower $T$ = peakier weights)
3. Ensemble forecasts: $\hat\pi_{s,w} = \sum_{s'} w_{s'} \hat\pi_{s',s,w}$

**Advantage:** Recognizes that historical seasons have diverse shapes (e.g., steep 2022–23 vs gradual 2017–18) and adapts which template to use without being forced into a single population shape.

**Tuned parameters (LOSO grid search):**
- `k_ref` = 25 (basis dimension for FS reference GAM)
- `multi_temperature` = 0.5 (strongly peaks weights on best-matching template)
- `template_shift` = −1 (corrects ~1-week detection lag)
- `align_rise_weight` = 1.0 (uniform loss weights across all phases — time-weighting not beneficial)

| Parameter | Symbol | Interpretation | Flexibility |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Shift** | $\tau_w$ | Time shift of epidemic centre relative to template | Always fitted |
| **Dilation** | $\delta_w$ | Stretch ($> 0$) or compression ($< 0$) of timeline | Fixed at 0 early; activated at `WEEK_THRESHOLD_DELTA` |
| **Intercept** | $a_w$ | Overall positivity level | Always fitted |
| **Amplitude** | $b_w$ | Scale of rise/fall | Fixed at 1 early (`allow_scale=FALSE`); released when threshold crossed |

**Progressive activation guards** prevent unstable early-season fits:
- `allow_scale = FALSE` (early): Only $a_w$ estimated, $b_w = 1$ fixed
- `delta_on = FALSE` (early): Only $\tau_w$ estimated, $\delta_w = 0$ fixed

The peak is derived from the fitted parameters as:
$$t_{\mathrm{peak},w} = \tau_w + (1+\delta_w)\cdot u^*, \quad \text{converted back to calendar: } w_s^p = t_{\mathrm{peak},w} - w^{an} + \hat{w}^{(\mathrm{ign})}_s$$
where $u^*$ is the argmax of $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$.

### Post-peak flag tuning {#sec-peak-tuning}

The parameters governing when the peak is declared *passed* are tuned offline. `tune_peak_detection()` finds the combination that minimises detection delay with zero false positives:

| Parameter | Role |
|-----------|------|
| `use_ci` | If `TRUE`, use upper CI of peak estimate (more conservative) |
| `buffer_weeks` | Extra weeks beyond threshold before declaring peak passed |

**Key functions:** `tune_peak_detection()`

**Full analysis:** [`peak_detection_tuning.html`](peak_detection_tuning.html)

### Training {#sec-m1-training}

Reference curve estimation (see §@sec-refcurve), multi-template alignment methods, and peak detection parameters are all validated via LOSO cross-validation to prevent data leakage. Post-peak flag tuning uses the alignment outputs produced by LOSO — no separate alignment pass is needed.

#### LOSO walk-forward evaluation {#sec-loso}

Reference curve methods, multi-template ensemble parameters, and alignment performance are validated via leave-one-season-out (LOSO) cross-validation to prevent data leakage.

**Extended grid search:** A 4-dimensional grid of M1 hyperparameters is evaluated:
- `k_ref`: {15, 20, 25, 30, 35} — reference curve basis dimension
- `multi_temperature`: {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} — softmax concentration on best template
- `template_shift`: {−2, −1, 0, 1} — offset to correct detection lag
- `align_rise_weight`: {1.0} — uniform loss emphasis (time-weighting not tuned)

For each configuration in the grid, LOSO evaluation proceeds:

1. Re-run `estimateRef(method="fs")` + `learn_alignment_hyperparams()` on training seasons only (test season excluded)
2. Detect ignition on test season prospectively using tuned M0 thresholds
3. Walk forward week by week, calling `run_alignment_prospective()` or `align_multi_template()` at each step
4. Collect `params_df` with fitted parameters and alignment outputs at each week

Peak MAE is computed under **Weibull weighting** (emphasizes near-peak predictions where uncertainty matters most) and is the primary tuning metric. The resulting `params_df` is consumed directly by M2 training — no second alignment pass is needed.

**Selected configuration (baseline grid, 81 configs):**
- k_ref=25, temperature=0.5, shift=−1 → Weibull MAE = 1.2178 weeks

**Extended grid:** 36 new boundary-pushing configurations in progress to verify whether edge optima can be improved.

**Key functions:** `loso_walkforward()`, `tune_m1_alignment()`

**Full analysis:** [`loso_walkforward.html`](loso_walkforward.html)

### Deployment: Weekly prospective alignment {#sec-run}

Each week, either `run_alignment_prospective()` (single-template) or `align_multi_template()` (multi-template) is called with frozen training products:

- `ref` — reference curve + per-season curves `eta_mat` from FS method
- `hyper` — alignment hyperparameters
- `params` — ignition thresholds from M0 training
- `multi_temperature`, `template_shift` — multi-template ensemble parameters
- `use_ci`, `buffer_weeks` — peak detection settings

**Multi-template flow (selected):**
1. Align current-season data to each template in `eta_mat` → collect {$\tau_s', \delta_s', a_s', b_s'$, NLL$_s'$} for each training season $s'$
2. Compute softmax weights from NLL values with tuned temperature
3. Ensemble forecasts and peak estimate across templates

**Single-template flow (fallback):**
1. Align to population reference $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$ only

The function returns:
- Current **state**: `"pre_ignition"` / `"aligning"` / `"post_peak"`
- Estimated peak week with CI
- Aligned forecast trajectory
- (Multi-template only) Per-template parameters and softmax weights

Once `state == "post_peak"`, alignment freezes and the peak estimate is final.

**Key functions:** `run_alignment_prospective()`, `align_multi_template()`

**Full analysis:** [`loso_walkforward.html`](loso_walkforward.html), [`run.html`](run.html)

---

## $M_2$ — Forecast Model {#sec-m2}

**Goal:** forecast weekly percentage positivity 1–2 weeks ahead using alignment outputs and prospective derivatives.

$M_2$ receives alignment outputs **already in aligned-week space** — it never
operates on raw `weekF` directly. The reference curve $g_\text{ref}$ is
injected as one covariate evaluated at $w^a_{s,w}$, not re-estimated here.

| Alignment input (from §@sec-m1 / @sec-run) | Role in $M_2$ |
|-------------------|---------------|
| $w^a_{s,w}$ (`newWeek`) | time axis for all GAM smooths; defines "where in the epidemic" |
| $\tau_w, \delta_w$ (time shift, dilation) | coarse alignment already absorbed into $w^a_{s,w}$ |
| $a_w, b_w$ (intercept, amplitude) | season level and intensity from M₁ fit |
| `state` (`aligning` / `post_peak`) | gates whether forecasting is active |
| $w_s^p$ (`peak_weekF`) with CI | explicit peak-timing anchor |

### Training {#sec-m2-training}

$M_2$ fits a **joint** binomial GAM for both horizons $h \in \{1,2\}$ simultaneously, with data stacked across leads and all smooth terms lead-specific. Observations are down-weighted exponentially by time since ignition ($\lambda_w \ge 0$) to emphasise early post-ignition weeks.

**Covariate sources — what comes from where:**

| Covariate | Source | Leakage-sensitive? |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| $w^a_{s,w}$ (`newWeek`) — aligned time axis | M1: alignment via ignition week | Yes — depends on $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$ |
| $z_{s,w}(\delta, Kr)$ — ramped template backbone | M1: $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$ evaluated in aligned space | Yes — **is** $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$ |
| $z^{(\mathrm{ema})}_{s,w}$ — EWMA positivity level | Raw data: computed by `prep_stage2_joint()` from $p = y/N$ | No |
| $d1_{s,w}$ — slope (logit scale) | Raw data: from `estimateDerivs()` on observed $p$ | No |
| $d2_{s,w}$ — curvature (logit scale) | Raw data: from `estimateDerivs()` on observed $p$ | No |
| $S_2^h(w^a_{s,w})$ — global aligned-time smooth | M1: operates on `newWeek` | Yes — depends on alignment |
| $S_{3,s}^h(w^a_{s,w})$ — season deviation smooth | M1: operates on `newWeek` | Yes — depends on alignment |

The EWMA, slope, and curvature are computed from real-time surveillance data — they do not pass through M1's alignment. The leakage-sensitive covariates are the aligned time axis and the reference curve template, both of which depend on which seasons were used to build $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$.

**LOSO training with leakage-free alignment.** M2's LOSO calls `prep_stage2_joint()` on `alignedD` split into train/test seasons. The alignment-derived columns (`newWeek`, template) come from a reference curve that must exclude the held-out season to prevent leakage. This is ensured by using the `alignedD` produced from LOSO-fold-specific reference curves. The real-time covariates ($z^{(\mathrm{ema})}$, $d1$, $d2$) are recomputed from raw data within `prep_stage2_joint()` and do not leak.

The nested LOSO structure, and what varies at each level:

```
For each candidate hyperparameter set (δ, Kr, α_state, λ_w):        ← tuned
  For each held-out season s*:                                         ← LOSO fold
    Fixed:   g_ref estimated without s*  → determines newWeek and template
    Recomputed: z_ema, d1, d2 from raw y/N by prep_stage2_joint()
    Trained: GAM coefficients on alignedD rows for s ≠ s*
    Predict: alignedD rows for s*, treating s* as a new season
             (b_{s*} and S^h_{3,s*} excluded from linear predictor)
  Evaluate: mean NLL over first W_eval=12 post-ignition weeks (uniform weights)
Select: hyperparameter set with lowest mean NLL across folds
```

| Parameter | Role |
|-----------|------|
| $\delta$ | Integer-week horizontal nudge applied to the template |
| $Kr$ | Ramp length — weeks over which the template is phased in after ignition |
| $\alpha_\text{state}$ | EWMA decay rate |
| $\lambda_w$ | Training time-decay weight (governs learning emphasis, not the LOSO criterion) |

**Key functions:** `prep_stage2_joint()`, `train_stage2_joint()`, `tune_stage2_loso_spec_grid_parallel()`

**Full design:** [`forecast_training.html`](forecast_training.html)

### Deployment: Weekly refit {#sec-m2-deploy}

Each week after ignition, $M_2$ is refit on the historical dataset augmented with current-season rows up to week $w$. All hyperparameters are frozen at LOSO-tuned values; only GAM coefficients are updated. As current-season data accumulate, the season random effect and season-specific shape smooth self-correct each week.

$M_2$ runs **after** $M_1$ each week: `newWeek` and the template covariate require the alignment state that $M_1$ just produced, while EWMA, slope, and curvature are recomputed from the latest raw surveillance data by `prep_stage2_joint()`.

**Key functions:** `refit_stage2_weekly()`, `stage2_predict_series()`

---

## Integration: Deployment sequence {#sec-train-deploy}

**Goal:** clarify how $M_1$ (alignment) and $M_2$ (forecast model) are connected
week to week, and how $M_2$ is trained correctly in a walk-forward regime without
data leakage. The full working implementation is in [`prospective_deployment.html`](prospective_deployment.html).

### Week-by-week execution

$M_1$ and $M_2$ run **sequentially within each week**:

1. **$M_1$ first.** `run_alignment_prospective()` processes all data up to
   the current week $w$ and returns the alignment state plus aligned covariates
   ($w^a_{s,w}$, $\tau_w$, $\delta_w$, $a_w$, $b_w$, `state`, peak estimate
   with CI).
2. **$M_2$ second.** Only if `state == "aligning"`, $M_2$ receives those outputs,
   appends the current-season row to $\mathcal{D}_\text{hist}$, and calls
   `refit_stage2_weekly()` to update the GAM coefficients before producing
   forecasts $\hat\pi_{s,w+1}$ and $\hat\pi_{s,w+2}$.

$M_2$ **cannot run before $M_1$** in a given week because the aligned time axis
$w^a_{s,w}$ and template covariate require the alignment state that $M_1$ just
produced. The EWMA, slope, and curvature are recomputed from the latest raw data
by `prep_stage2_joint()`, but they still need the post-ignition filter that
depends on $M_1$'s ignition call. All hyperparameters remain frozen (LOSO-tuned);
only the GAM coefficients change as new rows accumulate.

```
Week w arrives
  └─ M₁:  align_multi_template(currentSeason[1:w], eta_mat, g_ref, hyper, …)
         └─ for each training season s':
              align_forecast_pipeline_dilate(…, time_weights, temperature, …)
              → {τ_s', δ_s', a_s', b_s', NLL_s'}
         └─ compute softmax weights w_s' = exp(−NLL_s'/T) / Σ
         └─ ensemble: state, w^a_{s,w}, peak_weekF (+ CI)
              └─ if state == "aligning":
                   M₂:  refit_stage2_weekly(D_hist ∪ {row w with M₁ outputs})
                         └─ predict: π̂_{s,w+1},  π̂_{s,w+2}
```

The alignment loss is computed with **time-weighted observations** emphasizing ignition-to-peak weeks, then weighted by template quality (NLL) via softmax.

This cycle repeats every week until `state == "post_peak"`, at which point
alignment freezes and $M_2$ can be called one final time or retired for the
season.

### Leakage-free $M_2$ training

The nested LOSO design that prevents data leakage is described in full in §@sec-m2-training. In brief: M2's leakage-sensitive covariates (aligned time axis `newWeek` and the template backbone $z_{s,w}$) depend on the reference curve $g_{\mathrm{ref}}$, so M2's LOSO must use a reference curve that excludes the held-out season. Real-time covariates (EWMA, slope, curvature) are computed from raw surveillance data and do not leak.

### Full pipeline execution order

The table below summarises what is done offline (once, before the live season
begins) and what is repeated online (every week during the live season).

| Step | Component | When | What freezes |
|------|-----------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Reference curve + `hyper` (`estimateRef`, `learn_alignment_hyperparams`) | Offline, once | @sec-refcurve |
| 2 | Ignition detector thresholds $\hat\theta$ | Offline, once | @sec-m0 |
| 3 | LOSO walk-forward ($M_1$) → `params_df` | Offline, once | @sec-loso |
| 4 | Post-peak flag parameters (`use_ci`, `buffer_weeks`) | Offline, once | §@sec-peak-tuning |
| 5 | $M_2$ hyperparameter tuning ($\delta$, $Kr$, $\alpha_\text{state}$, $\lambda_w$) | Offline, once | @sec-m2 |
| 6 | $M_2$ initial fit on full historical `params_df` (`train_stage2_joint`) | Offline, before season start | GAM structure |
| **7** | **Weekly: $M_1$ → $M_2$ refit → forecast** | **Online, every week** | GAM coefficients only |

Steps 1–6 are completed once and the products are frozen objects (`ref`, `hyper`,
`params`, `use_ci`, `buffer_weeks`, and the initial GAM fit). Step 7 is the only
online component: $M_1$ runs first each week to produce alignment outputs, then
$M_2$ is immediately refit and used to generate forecasts. The whole cycle is
weekly, not streaming real-time.

---

## Document map {#sec-docmap}

| Document | What it covers |
|----------|---------------|
| [`estimateRef.html`](estimateRef.html) | §@sec-refcurve — 6 reference curve methods, FS selection, per-season curves |
| [`ignition_training.html`](ignition_training.html) | §@sec-m0 — ignition gate definitions and LOSO threshold tuning |
| **[`loso_walkforward.html`](loso_walkforward.html)** | **§@sec-loso — Multi-template ensemble: grid search tuning, extended boundary exploration, per-season plots** |
| [`peak_detection_tuning.html`](peak_detection_tuning.html) | §@sec-peak-tuning — post-peak flag parameter tuning |
| [`forecast_training.html`](forecast_training.html) | §@sec-m2 — $M_2$ forecast model specification, training, and nested LOSO hyperparameter tuning |
| [`m1_m2_stacking.html`](m1_m2_stacking.html) | $M_1 \to M_2$ stacking — replacing static template with walk-forward $M_1$ predictions |
| [`prospective_deployment.html`](prospective_deployment.html) | **Full deployment** — M0→M1→M2 walk-forward pipeline for the current season, with forecast evolution visualizations |
| [`run.html`](run.html) | Current season M1-only prospective alignment (legacy; no M2 forecasts) |
| *(this document)* | Overview — notation, stage descriptions, deployment sequence, multi-template architecture |

---

## Key design choices

**Multi-template ensemble alignment.** Instead of forcing all seasons into one population reference shape, the FS reference method produces per-season curves. M₁ aligns to each training season's curve independently and ensembles forecasts via softmax weighting on NLL. This allows the model to recognize which historical season the current one most resembles (e.g., steep 2022–23 vs gradual 2017–18) without being constrained to a single population template. Temperature controls how strongly the model commits to the best-matching template.

**Weekly refit without leakage.** $M_2$ is refit every week by appending the
growing current season to the fixed historical dataset. All modelling happens
in aligned-week space ($w^a_{s,w}$) — the GAM smooth terms $S_2^h$ and
$S_{3,s}^h$ provide additional flexibility to shift and stretch the forecast
curve beyond M₁'s coarse alignment, so the model is not rigidly constrained
to follow $g_\text{ref}$. Hyperparameters are frozen at LOSO-tuned values;
only GAM coefficients update each week. The season random effect and
season-specific factor-smooth absorb both level and shape corrections as
current-season data accumulate.

**Reference curve uses known labels (@sec-refcurve before @sec-m0).** The reference
curve (and per-season FS curves) are estimated from retrospectively labelled ignition weeks — not from
the prospective detector. This separation means the templates are not corrupted
by detection errors, and the prospective detector is evaluated independently.

**Stateless prospective function.** `align_multi_template()` and `run_alignment_prospective()` take the
current data and return the current state — the caller decides whether to keep
calling. The same functions power both the LOSO inner loop and production
deployment, guaranteeing identical behaviour.

**LOSO grid search for multi-template tuning.** All M₁ tuning (`k_ref`, `multi_temperature`, `template_shift`)
uses walk-forward LOSO so that no future data leaks into training. A 4×3×4 grid (baseline 81 configs, extended ~120 configs) is evaluated
to find the peak MAE minimizer under Weibull weighting. Ignition
detector thresholds are tuned in an earlier LOSO pass (@sec-m0).

**Alignment stops at peak.** Once `peak_passed == TRUE`, alignment freezes.
This prevents the descending limb from distorting the peak estimate, and
cleanly separates the peak prediction module from any downstream forecasting.

**Two-stage identifiability guard.** Two parameters are activated progressively
as data accumulate. The slope $b_w$ is fixed at 1 (`allow_scale = FALSE`) until
sufficient data span the curve. The dilation $\delta_w$ is fixed at 0
(`delta_on = FALSE`) until a further threshold is crossed. This prevents
numerically unstable estimates early in the season when few observations are
available.
